title: MeshNode - POE Mesh Transmitter with 1W Sx1262 Transceiver, Outdoor Housing, N Type and Ethernet!

messages.author: Jack @ PCBA.UK
messages.text: Hi everyone! I have been working on a little project over the past couple of weeks and would like to share with you as I might manufacturer a small run of these if there is demand for such a device. I have been after setting up a node at a few locations for a while but I am not a fan of building a box and having to mount that up a pole with wires hanging out. So I have come up with a transceiver design that is inspired by the Ubiquiti Bullet AP range where you have a transceiver directly mount to the base of an antenna. This has a few advantages, but mainly that there is very little loss between the transceiver chip and the antenna. It also looks a lot neater than having a project box mounted to the pole and wires hanging out... Even though they are fun to make. I have gone with a 1W transceiver so if you need to mount it inside, on a long cable, you still have that option. The 30dB power will run through a good few meters of even the cheap cable and you will still be able to transmit at the max allowed EIRP. I trust that none of you will use the full 30dB and a high gain antenna to get longer range...... Will have 2 variants, 868MHz and 915MHz. Could also do a 443MHz on request though that uses a different radio. The main processor is an ESP32 P4. Lots of power in these so you can run the MeshCore nativity or run with other software. More info in the Software section. The main feature of this is the Ethernet port. You can just connect it to any 802.3af injector/switch and the unit has power. These can run upwards of 250m with long range POE hardware. 10/100Mbps network allow for you to use any native networking functions in MeshCore or custom firmware. I would love to get the housing injection moulded. The economics of doing it means we would have to make quite a few as the moulds are quite expensive. If we do a small run, it will be 3D printed in ASA. The PCB will be assembled in the UK in my factory! https://meshnode.tech/ (edited)

messages.author: Jack @ PCBA.UK
messages.text: Software The POC software I have vibe coded is just Meshcore embedded into a custom firmware stack. It is not a rewrite or anything. You can run whatever version you want and it just builds it as a single firmware with the extended features on top. No changes to the MeshCore firmware at all. Which is a major advantage of MeshCore over other mesh implementations! It extends the MeshCore base class and adds Ethernet management, a web UI, REST API, OTA updates, persistent neighbor logging, MQTT reporting, and LetsMesh integration on top of MeshCore. You can run MeshCore by its self without the custom firmware but there is no Ethernet support YET so you wont be able to use the networking feature. (edited)
messages.content_html:
Software

The POC software I have vibe coded is just Meshcore embedded into a custom firmware stack. It is not a rewrite or anything. You can run whatever version you want and it just builds it as a single firmware with the extended features on top. No changes to the MeshCore firmware at all. Which is a major advantage of MeshCore over other mesh implementations! It extends the MeshCore base class and adds Ethernet management, a web UI, REST API, OTA updates, persistent neighbor logging, MQTT reporting, and LetsMesh integration on top of MeshCore. You can run MeshCore by its self without the custom firmware but there is no Ethernet support YET so you wont be able to use the networking feature. (edited)

messages.author: Jack @ PCBA.UK
messages.text: Antennas The MeshBullet uses N Type antenna connection. I have a good source for these antennas from China which I have used for 10+ years now. Would be happy to bundle the hardware with an antenna that has the correct N Type fitting. The 868MHz options are: 85cm 9.5dBi 55cm 8dBi 40cm 5.5dBi 27cm 3dBi (edited)
messages.content_html:
Antennas

The MeshBullet uses N Type antenna connection. I have a good source for these antennas from China which I have used for 10+ years now. Would be happy to bundle the hardware with an antenna that has the correct N Type fitting. The 868MHz options are:
  • 85cm 9.5dBi
  • 55cm 8dBi
  • 40cm 5.5dBi
  • 27cm 3dBi
 (edited)

messages.author: Jack @ PCBA.UK
messages.text: Variants Could also take this housing design and run smaller cheaper radios in it with battery for ad hoc deployments. 5-12v DC input on the bottom instead of RJ45 so you can connect to a solar charger. SMA antenna instead of N Type. If we got the housing injection moulded, this could be a super cheap kit for those sorts of node deployments where you don't know if someone is going to steal the node or not... (edited)
messages.content_html:
Variants

Could also take this housing design and run smaller cheaper radios in it with battery for ad hoc deployments. 5-12v DC input on the bottom instead of RJ45 so you can connect to a solar charger. SMA antenna instead of N Type. If we got the housing injection moulded, this could be a super cheap kit for those sorts of node deployments where you don't know if someone is going to steal the node or not... (edited)

messages.author: ka.RFz
messages.text: Very cool, really interested in the idea. I have always liked the Ubiquiti Bullet M series and have wanted a similar form factor of radio for Meshcore. In my region there are many amateur radio repeater sites with emergency power in the shack, so going with Solar nodes and tower mounted boxes isn't very practical. Hanging something like this off the bottom of an omni with a PoE feed back to the shack makes a lot more sense.
messages.content_html: Very cool, really interested in the idea. I have always liked the Ubiquiti Bullet M series and have wanted a similar form factor of radio for Meshcore. In my region there are many amateur radio repeater sites with emergency power in the shack, so going with Solar nodes and tower mounted boxes isn't very practical. Hanging something like this off the bottom of an omni with a PoE feed back to the shack makes a lot more sense.

messages.author: Jack @ PCBA.UK
messages.text: Little preview of the solar node internals. Note I am only doing the POE at launch but because the housing will be also used for solar node, we are designing it to handle the battery for this version of the device which means prototyping the battery assembly while we are also working on POE... Little extra pre planning allows for easier time making the solar version and means no case changes, which would be $$$$. 25Wh (6500mAh) enough for you guys? Looking at roughly 2-3 weeks as a repeater without power input with the 22dBm radio. (edited)
messages.content_html: Little preview of the solar node internals. Note I am only doing the POE at launch but because the housing will be also used for solar node, we are designing it to handle the battery for this version of the device which means prototyping the battery assembly while we are also working on POE... Little extra pre planning allows for easier time making the solar version and means no case changes, which would be $$$$. 25Wh (6500mAh) enough for you guys? Looking at roughly 2-3 weeks as a repeater without power input with the 22dBm radio. (edited)

messages.author: Jack @ PCBA.UK
messages.text: Would love to get some feedback on IO ports on both the POE and Solar nodes. Thinking of doing an internal I2C header and JST connector with RS-485 on it for external applications. The cable gland we are looking to use can support 1-2 cables so would allow for power (plus network on POE) and a external sensor over RS-485 on another cable.
messages.content_html: Would love to get some feedback on IO ports on both the POE and Solar nodes. Thinking of doing an internal I2C header and JST connector with RS-485 on it for external applications. The cable gland we are looking to use can support 1-2 cables so would allow for power (plus network on POE) and a external sensor over RS-485 on another cable.
messages.reactions_html: 1

messages.author: KA5PLE (Andy)
messages.text: Rs485 would generally be a great idea, even if it’s just to have other devices connect to the node serial. BUT as a letter to Santa: Please make it bidirectional. Writing the code is one thing, not having the needed hardware is an entirely different problem to deal with
messages.content_html: Rs485 would generally be a great idea, even if it’s just to have other devices connect to the node serial. BUT as a letter to Santa: Please make it bidirectional. Writing the code is one thing, not having the needed hardware is an entirely different problem to deal with
messages.reply_html:
Jack @ PCBA.UK
Would love to get some feedback on IO ports on both the POE and Solar nodes. Thinking of doing an internal I2C header and JST connector with RS-485 on it for external applications. The cable gland we are looking to use can support 1-2 cables so would allow for power (plus network on POE) and a external sensor over RS-485 on another cable.

messages.author: KA5PLE (Andy)
messages.text: Full duplex is more than likely not needed. Or let me put that a different way: I have not seen RS485 sensors that run full duplex. But half duplex (2 wire) bidirectional
messages.content_html: Full duplex is more than likely not needed. Or let me put that a different way: I have not seen RS485 sensors that run full duplex. But half duplex (2 wire) bidirectional

Jack @ PCBA.UK

messages.author: Jack @ PCBA.UK
messages.text: Then plan is half duplex with ground and +5v.
messages.content_html: Then plan is half duplex with ground and +5v.
messages.reply_html:
KA5PLE (Andy)
Full duplex is more than likely not needed. Or let me put that a different way: I have not seen RS485 sensors that run full duplex. But half duplex (2 wire) bidirectional

messages.author: KA5PLE (Andy)
messages.text: Fine by me, but that is not RS485 as a I know it.
messages.content_html: Fine by me, but that is not RS485 as a I know it.

messages.author: Jack @ PCBA.UK
messages.text: The idea is to provide a way for remote sensors to connect to the node if the users need it. The chips have I2C, UART and SPI. These only go a few feet AFAIK so RS485 would be better for longer reach. The power is then power the remote sensor if it needs it.
messages.content_html: The idea is to provide a way for remote sensors to connect to the node if the users need it. The chips have I2C, UART and SPI. These only go a few feet AFAIK so RS485 would be better for longer reach. The power is then power the remote sensor if it needs it.

messages.author: KA5PLE (Andy)
messages.text: Yeah I understand now. As long as the RS485 is a real differential driver that would be perfect.
messages.content_html: Yeah I understand now. As long as the RS485 is a real differential driver that would be perfect.
messages.reactions_html: 1

messages.reply_html:
Jack @ PCBA.UK
Little preview of the solar node internals. Note I am only doing the POE at launch but because the housing will be also used for solar node, we are designing it to handle the battery for this version of the device which means prototyping the battery assembly while we are also working on POE... Little extra pre planning allows for easier time making the solar version and means no case changes, which would be $$$$. 25Wh (6500mAh) enough for you guys? Looking at roughly 2-3 weeks as a repeater without power input with the 22dBm radio. (edited)

Would love to get some feedback on IO ports on both the POE and Solar nodes. Thinking of doing an internal I2C header and JST connector with RS-485 on it for external applications. The cable gland we are looking to use can support 1-2 cables so would allow for power (plus network on POE) and a external sensor over RS-485 on another cable.

messages.author: Jack @ PCBA.UK
messages.text: Adding some headers to the POE version. What would you like? So far I have UART for debugging the P4 (not soldered) Qwiic connector for Sparkfun I2C devices and a 2mm HC-HY header for a 2nd I2C (pinout supports Grove products) Might add a dedicated GPS header (not soldered) with 5V, 2 data IOs and a reset IO? I don't have a lot of room left, though there are a few more GPIO pins available. Will probably have onboard sensor for temp though this will be for onboard monitoring of the device to give a rough idea of temp. Let me know! Note we have a decent amount of 5V rail left and I added a decent 2A buck for 3.3v so there is probably 1.5A on that left for add on devices. Though the MeshNode POE is primarily designed as a repeater, these headers are <$1 and will allow you to add features of your own. (that and it is a "dev board" not a retail product) (edited)
messages.content_html: Adding some headers to the POE version. What would you like? So far I have UART for debugging the P4 (not soldered) Qwiic connector for Sparkfun I2C devices and a 2mm HC-HY header for a 2nd I2C (pinout supports Grove products) Might add a dedicated GPS header (not soldered) with 5V, 2 data IOs and a reset IO? I don't have a lot of room left, though there are a few more GPIO pins available. Will probably have onboard sensor for temp though this will be for onboard monitoring of the device to give a rough idea of temp. Let me know! Note we have a decent amount of 5V rail left and I added a decent 2A buck for 3.3v so there is probably 1.5A on that left for add on devices. Though the MeshNode POE is primarily designed as a repeater, these headers are <$1 and will allow you to add features of your own. (that and it is a "dev board" not a retail product) (edited)

messages.author: Mike WB6KTN
messages.text: Which ethernet chip are you using on your board? MeshCore does not currently support any of the common i2c ethernet controllers like the W5100S that MeshTastic does. Consider PPS support if you add GPS. The third data pin that you called "reset" could work if it can be configured as an input AND it is routed to an interrupt controller unless your SOC can generate an interrupt from every GPIO pin.
messages.content_html: Which ethernet chip are you using on your board? MeshCore does not currently support any of the common i2c ethernet controllers like the W5100S that MeshTastic does. Consider PPS support if you add GPS. The third data pin that you called "reset" could work if it can be configured as an input AND it is routed to an interrupt controller unless your SOC can generate an interrupt from every GPIO pin.

messages.author: ccw
messages.text: PPS would be cool
messages.content_html: PPS would be cool

messages.author: Mike WB6KTN
messages.text: It is not clear to me your use case. I have multiple PoE switches in my house for remote ethernet switches, WiFi access points and cameras. I acquired a wisblock with PoE for a MT roof node. I so wanted to have a node that was fully controlled from my chair. But.. While the MT app does support the ethernet chip there is no client that will connect. It is an incomplete solution. Then I switched to MC which does not support ethernet. That is, I can't sit in my chair and configure my repeater and look at its stats on this project either. So, that node is now in my shed running on solar power, just because I can. All of my PoE switches are power hogs when you have a total power budget of 10 watts. (edited)
messages.content_html: It is not clear to me your use case. I have multiple PoE switches in my house for remote ethernet switches, WiFi access points and cameras. I acquired a wisblock with PoE for a MT roof node. I so wanted to have a node that was fully controlled from my chair. But.. While the MT app does support the ethernet chip there is no client that will connect. It is an incomplete solution. Then I switched to MC which does not support ethernet. That is, I can't sit in my chair and configure my repeater and look at its stats on this project either. So, that node is now in my shed running on solar power, just because I can. All of my PoE switches are power hogs when you have a total power budget of 10 watts. (edited)

messages.author: Jack @ PCBA.UK
messages.text: My POC software runs on top of MeshCore and adds support for networking, MQTT, NTP, the web UI control etc. Personally I think it is better to keep MeshCore to its core values and add features on top for added uses such as this product or others. Not saying it should not add Ethernet support but it does not need to do everything. (edited)
messages.content_html: My POC software runs on top of MeshCore and adds support for networking, MQTT, NTP, the web UI control etc. Personally I think it is better to keep MeshCore to its core values and add features on top for added uses such as this product or others. Not saying it should not add Ethernet support but it does not need to do everything. (edited)
messages.reply_html:
Mike WB6KTN
Which ethernet chip are you using on your board? MeshCore does not currently support any of the common i2c ethernet controllers like the W5100S that MeshTastic does. Consider PPS support if you add GPS. The third data pin that you called "reset" could work if it can be configured as an input AND it is routed to an interrupt controller unless your SOC can generate an interrupt from every GPIO pin.

messages.author: Jack @ PCBA.UK
messages.text: That is why you have to build on top of the MeshCore as all it is, is a "routing mesh protocol". Those apps build on top of MeshCore by extracting out the UI and features into their own thing. It keeps the project light weight and easy to run, develope and deploy. Which is what you want in a low power mesh network. I have been able to take MeshCore, add the features we need to make this product work and from the sounds of it what you want which is remote management, without the app, on your PC. Basically made a web app that communicates over ethernet rather than Bluetooth or serial but it is built into the same hardware that runs MeshCore.
messages.content_html: That is why you have to build on top of the MeshCore as all it is, is a "routing mesh protocol". Those apps build on top of MeshCore by extracting out the UI and features into their own thing. It keeps the project light weight and easy to run, develope and deploy. Which is what you want in a low power mesh network. I have been able to take MeshCore, add the features we need to make this product work and from the sounds of it what you want which is remote management, without the app, on your PC. Basically made a web app that communicates over ethernet rather than Bluetooth or serial but it is built into the same hardware that runs MeshCore.

I so wanted to have a node that was fully controlled from my chair. But.. While the MT app does support the ethernet chip there is no client that will connect. It is an incomplete solution. Then I switched to MC which does not support ethernet. That is, I can't sit in my chair and configure my repeater and look at its stats on this project either. So, that node is now in my shed running on solar power, just because I can. All of my PoE switches are power hogs when you have a total power budget of 10 watts. (edited)

embeds_html:
Support for RAK ethernet module by rgregg · Pull Request #1983 · ...
This pull request adds Ethernet support to the companion_radio, simple_repeater, and simple_room_server examples for nRF52-based devices, enabling TCP CLI access and mesh command handling over Ethe...

messages.author: Mike WB6KTN
messages.text: @Jack @ PCBA.UK Sounds like fun. As long as you are satisfied using the RJ45 with PoE for power only. It turned out that, at least for me, it was easier for me to power the node via USB where I put mine. I was able to make 5v from the 12v battery easily enough but getting 50 volts for the PoE injector would have been work. That and you still have to pull the node down from the roof to upgrade it. I have little doubt that ethernet support will eventually be added to MC permitting a node to run without stray rf (wifi/bt).
messages.content_html: @Jack @ PCBA.UK Sounds like fun. As long as you are satisfied using the RJ45 with PoE for power only. It turned out that, at least for me, it was easier for me to power the node via USB where I put mine. I was able to make 5v from the 12v battery easily enough but getting 50 volts for the PoE injector would have been work. That and you still have to pull the node down from the roof to upgrade it. I have little doubt that ethernet support will eventually be added to MC permitting a node to run without stray rf (wifi/bt).

messages.author: ka.RFz
messages.text: These are similarly sized to the Ubiquiti Bullets / MikroTik Groove. 99% of the time we hang them those models off the antenna, with the antenna mounted to a mast or similar. Many of the fiberglass and similar omni's come with mounting hardware already. But yeah could be problematic for smaller antennas that don't come with mounting hardware.

messages.author: bluebrolly.
messages.text: The cap should screw into the main unit from inside. The way you currently have it, if you have a bad seal water can get into the device and accumulate. Having it the other way around, you dont need a gasket and theres no way for water to get in (edited)
messages.content_html: The cap should screw into the main unit from inside. The way you currently have it, if you have a bad seal water can get into the device and accumulate. Having it the other way around, you dont need a gasket and theres no way for water to get in (edited)
messages.reply_html:
bluebrolly.
The threading should be reversed.
